title: Git Foundations
subtitle: Build one project history step by step, then prove the same Git habits in new repositories.
description: A beginner local-Git course organized around three cumulative repositories. Guided lessons grow one community-site repository across the whole course, module projects continue one parallel volunteer-hub repository, and the final capstone starts a fresh neighborhood-guide repository. Learners inspect, change, and verify repository state without branches, remotes, GitHub, or assumed web-development knowledge.
prerequisites: Complete Linux Terminal Fundamentals or be comfortable using pwd, ls, cd, mkdir, touch, cat, cp, mv, and rm in a terminal.
Know how to open, edit, and save a simple text file in the provided workspace.
No HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GitHub account, or previous version-control experience is required. Every required file edit supplies the exact text and location.
intendedFor: Beginners who can navigate a terminal and edit simple text files, and who want to understand local Git before learning branches, remotes, or GitHub collaboration.
learningGoals: Explain the working tree, staging area, commits, and HEAD using one project that grows across the course.
Initialize a repository, configure a repository-only identity, and read tracked, untracked, staged, modified, ignored, and clean state.
Inspect exact file changes, stage one purpose at a time, create meaningful commits, and read the resulting history.
Rename, remove, ignore, unstage, and restore files safely without rewriting history.
Reapply the same essential workflow in one cumulative module project and one fresh final capstone.

courseOverview.recommendedSequence: Follow the guided community-site repository in order, complete each volunteer-hub module-project milestone when it appears, then finish the fresh neighborhood-guide capstone.
courseOverview.summary: Learn Git through continuity rather than disposable exercises: one guided repository, one parallel module-project repository, and one independent capstone repository.

moduleSummary.title: Start the Community Site Repository
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Understand the local repository model, initialize on main, configure identity, read state, and record the first snapshot.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: community-site gains its first clean commit; volunteer-hub reaches its first clean snapshot.

moduleSummary.title: Build Meaningful History
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Inspect exact changes, stage one purpose, commit focused work, and read snapshots.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: community-site and volunteer-hub gain readable, focused histories without unrelated work mixed together.

moduleSummary.title: Maintain a Clean Repository
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Rename and remove tracked files, ignore generated output, and recover safely from working-tree and staging mistakes.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: community-site and volunteer-hub finish with intentional tracked files, useful ignore rules, and clean state.

moduleSummary.title: Final Capstone
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Rebuild the entire local workflow independently in a fresh repository.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: neighborhood-guide reaches a verified final handoff through six cumulative steps.

authoringGuidance: Use one cumulative guided repository named community-site for every technical teaching exercise in Modules 1-3.

Use one cumulative parallel module-project repository named volunteer-hub across all three module projects; Module 2 begins from Module 1’s canonical final state, and Module 3 begins from Module 2’s canonical final state.
Use one fresh cumulative capstone repository named neighborhood-guide for all six capstone steps.
Every exercise must be independently loadable: reconstruct the exact canonical prerequisite repository state in hidden setup rather than depending on a previous browser session.

For each cumulative stream, the canonical solution state of milestone N must equal the canonical starter state of milestone N+1, including files, commit history, staged state, unstaged state, ignored state, and terminal working directory.
This is a Git course, not a web-development course. Never assume HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Markdown, or shell-scripting knowledge beyond the stated prerequisites.

Whenever learners must edit a file, state the exact file path, the exact text to add or replace, and the exact insertion or replacement location. Grade the repository transition, not web syntax or design skill.
Keep file contents short, readable, and meaningful as Git changes. Do not ask learners to invent page structure, styling, scripts, copy, or filenames.
Teach each Git concept once in the guided stream. Later guided exercises, module projects, and the capstone may reuse it in a new repository state but must not reteach the same definition.
Keep setup state and grading expectations separate. Hidden setup creates the prerequisite milestone; gitExpectations and focused checks verify only the learner transition.
Use profileId git, fixedLanguage bash, recipeType shell_task, and mode terminal_workspace for every Git code_input.
Use the existing terminal-workspace runner and shared editor/file explorer. Do not create a Git-specific runner or expose hidden setup files.
Configure repository-local identity as ZoeSkoul Learner and learner@zoeskoul.local before the first commit.
Set the initial branch to main whenever initialization is part of the task, without turning main into a branching lesson.
Never grade exact hashes, timestamps, absolute workspace paths, or decorative log formatting.
Use gitExpectations for repository state and terminalExpectations only when the exact command is itself the learning goal.
Every terminal instruction must say to type the command and press Enter.
Keep the course local. Do not introduce branches, merges, remotes, GitHub, stash, reflog, revert, reset, rebase, tags, or force operations.
Use one synopsis sketch for each module project and capstone, followed by exactly the authored number of cumulative steps.
Write lessons like a practical book chapter: continue the project story, define one new Git idea, trace the state change, warn about one common mistake, and transition into practice.
Prepend one reading-only course introduction explaining prerequisites, the three repository journeys, the inspect-change-verify habit, and how deterministic resets preserve cumulative learning.
Keep quiz distractors inside the Git concepts already taught. Do not use branches, remotes, GitHub, merges, or other later-course features as easy wrong answers.
Avoid repeating the same concept check in later topics. Each quiz should assess the new repository layer or decision introduced by its teaching topic.

preferredTerms.run button: type the command and press Enter
preferredTerms.repository state: the working tree, staging area, and commit history Git currently records

notes: Use one cumulative guided repository named community-site for every technical teaching exercise in Modules 1-3.

Use one cumulative parallel module-project repository named volunteer-hub across all three module projects; Module 2 begins from Module 1’s canonical final state, and Module 3 begins from Module 2’s canonical final state.
Use one fresh cumulative capstone repository named neighborhood-guide for all six capstone steps.

Set the initial branch to main whenever initialization is part of the task, without turning main into a branching lesson.

Use gitExpectations for repository state and terminalExpectations only when the exact command is itself the learning goal.
Every terminal instruction must say to type the command and press Enter.
Keep the course local. Do not introduce branches, merges, remotes, GitHub, stash, reflog, revert, reset, rebase, tags, or force operations.
Use one synopsis sketch for each module project and capstone, followed by exactly the authored number of cumulative steps.
Write lessons like a practical book chapter: continue the project story, define one new Git idea, trace the state change, warn about one common mistake, and transition into practice.
Prepend one reading-only course introduction explaining prerequisites, the three repository journeys, the inspect-change-verify habit, and how deterministic resets preserve cumulative learning.
Prepend one reading-only course-introduction sketch titled A project needs a memory with no Try It.
Preserve the first technical sketch after the introduction instead of replacing it.
The introduction must establish the learner role, project problem, prerequisites, safe workspace, course journey, and final deliverable in natural prose.
Do not duplicate the outer topic title as the inner sketch title.
After the introduction, add a separate technical teaching sketch with a title beginning Worked example: and a body that explicitly begins Worked example:.

In that worked example, trace one project through an original file state, an accidental overwrite, and recovery from a recorded snapshot; contrast that reliable history with ambiguous final-copy folder names.
Keep the worked example command-free because this opening topic explains the problem Git solves before command instruction begins.
practice.conceptualOnly removes code_input practice for this topic; it does not remove the concrete worked-example walkthrough.

assessmentAndDelivery.suggestedBeginnerRhythm: Complete two lesson topics and their Try It exercises per study session, then finish each module with its cumulative repository project.
recommendedCourseDeliverables: Command-purpose learner quiz cards use non-code kinds; runtime Try It code_input remains in quizDraft authoring
assessmentAndDelivery.closingNote: Learners finish with a reliable local Git workflow and are ready to isolate work with branches.

modules.title: Start the Community Site Repository
modules.description: Begin with an ordinary community-site folder, initialize Git on main, configure a repository-only identity, read what Git sees, and record the first meaningful snapshot.
modules.purpose: Build the repository model in the order a beginner actually uses it: understand the problem, initialize, inspect state, stage, commit, and verify.
learningObjectives: Explain why Git history is safer and clearer than duplicate final folders.
Initialize the community-site folder on main and configure a repository-only identity.
Carry the same files from untracked state into a staged proposal and inspect that proposal.
Create and verify the first meaningful snapshot without mixing unrelated work.
guidedExercises: Turn the prepared community-site folder into a repository.
Carry the same five project files from untracked state into one staged proposal.
Record that proposal as the first commit and verify history plus clean state.
quizFocus: working tree and staging area state
modules.moduleProject: Continue the parallel volunteer-hub repository through five cumulative steps, ending with its first clear commit and a clean working tree.

sections.title: From Ordinary Folder to First Snapshot
sections.summary: Understand the problem Git solves, initialize your first repository, read its state, and move one deliberate change into history.

topics.title: Why Version Control Matters
topics.summary: Meet the three repositories used in this course, understand what Git records locally, and learn the inspect-change-verify habit before typing a Git command.
learningGoals: Explain what Git records locally and why project history is more useful than duplicate final folders.
Describe the guided, parallel-project, and capstone repository journeys.
Use inspect, change, and verify as the default course habit.

topics.title: Initialize the Community Site Repository
topics.summary: Turn the prepared community-site folder into a repository on main and store the course identity only inside that repository.
learningGoals: Initialize the existing community-site folder with main as its initial branch.
Explain that .git stores repository metadata without opening or editing it.
Configure the supplied author name and email only for community-site.

topics.title: Read the Community Site State
topics.summary: Carry the same community-site files from untracked state into one staged proposal, then inspect what Git would record.
learningGoals: Read the untracked community-site files with full status.
Stage the five exact project paths and interpret short status.
Inspect the staged snapshot summary without committing it.

topics.title: Record the First Community Site Snapshot
topics.summary: Carry the staged community-site proposal into its first commit, then verify the new history entry and clean state.
learningGoals: Record the already-staged community-site proposal with the exact first commit message.
Read the first concise history entry without changing repository state.
Verify that the committed working tree is clean.

sections.description: Begin with an ordinary community-site folder, initialize Git on main, configure a repository-only identity, read what Git sees, and record the first meaningful snapshot.

sections.title: Parallel Module Project
sections.summary: Start the cumulative volunteer-hub repository from an ordinary folder and record its first clean snapshot.

topics.title: Module Project: Start Volunteer Hub History
topics.summary: Begin the parallel volunteer-hub repository and create its first clean snapshot through five cumulative steps.
learningGoals: Initialize a real project folder on main before using later repository commands.
Configure a repository-only identity and distinguish untracked from staged files.
Create one meaningful initial commit and verify a clean working tree.

projectBrief.scenario: A volunteer coordinator has an ordinary volunteer-hub folder with a README and planning notes. Build its first trustworthy local snapshot.
projectBrief.role: Act as the person preparing the project for reliable local change tracking.
projectBrief.workspace: Use volunteer-hub for every step. This same repository continues in the Module 2 and Module 3 projects.
projectBrief.deliverable: One repository on main with local identity, one commit named Start volunteer hub, the authored files tracked, and a clean working tree.
requirements: Inspect the supplied folder before initialization.
Initialize volunteer-hub on main.
Configure the supplied local identity.
Stage only the authored project files.
Commit Start volunteer hub and verify clean state.

stepLadder.title: Inspect the Ordinary Folder
stepLadder.requirement: Inspect volunteer-hub and confirm README.md, index.html, styles.css, and notes/schedule.txt are present before Git is initialized.

stepLadder.title: Initialize the Repository
stepLadder.requirement: Initialize volunteer-hub with main as the initial branch.

stepLadder.title: Set the Local Identity
stepLadder.requirement: Configure ZoeSkoul Learner and learner@zoeskoul.local only inside volunteer-hub.

stepLadder.title: Stage the First Snapshot
stepLadder.requirement: Stage README.md, index.html, styles.css, and notes/schedule.txt, then inspect the proposed snapshot.

stepLadder.title: Commit and Verify
stepLadder.requirement: Commit Start volunteer hub, inspect the one-commit history, and confirm the working tree is clean.

sections.description: Continue the same volunteer-hub repository and reapply Module 1 without copying the guided community-site task.

modules.title: Build Meaningful History
modules.description: Continue community-site by inspecting exact edits, staging one purpose at a time, creating focused commits, and reading the snapshots already recorded.
modules.purpose: Turn file changes into understandable history without introducing another repository story or reteaching Module 1.
learningObjectives: Use git diff to inspect an exact unstaged text change before staging it.
Stage one purpose while leaving unrelated work outside the next commit.
Inspect staged changes, create a focused commit, and verify the remaining work.
Read concise history and inspect a committed snapshot with log and show.
guidedExercises: Inspect supplied HTML, CSS, and text edits without needing web-development knowledge.
Build two focused community-site commits from separate project purposes.
Use log and show to answer questions about the history just created.
modules.moduleProject: Continue volunteer-hub from Module 1 and build a focused two-change history through six cumulative steps.

sections.title: From Exact Edits to Focused History
sections.summary: Follow a small change from diff to staging to commit, then read the history it creates.

topics.title: Inspect Exact Project Changes
topics.summary: Continue community-site and inspect supplied text edits before deciding what belongs in history.
learningGoals: Inspect an exact supplied change with git diff before staging it.
Limit diff inspection to a named file when several files changed.
Read added and removed lines without needing HTML, CSS, or JavaScript knowledge.

topics.title: Stage One Purpose at a Time
topics.summary: Separate supplied community-site edits so each commit records one understandable project purpose.
learningGoals: Stage only the files that belong to one community-site purpose.
Inspect the staged snapshot separately from remaining working-tree changes.
Create a focused commit and verify that unrelated work remains outside it.

topics.title: Read the History You Built
topics.summary: Use concise history and committed-snapshot inspection to answer questions about community-site without changing it.
learningGoals: Read concise community-site history from newest to oldest.
Inspect the latest committed snapshot with git show.
Use history inspection without changing repository state.

sections.description: Continue community-site by inspecting exact edits, staging one purpose at a time, creating focused commits, and reading the snapshots already recorded.

sections.title: Parallel Module Project
sections.summary: Continue volunteer-hub with two exact file changes recorded as separate focused commits.

topics.title: Module Project: Build Volunteer Hub History
topics.summary: Continue the same volunteer-hub repository from Module 1 and create focused content and style commits through six cumulative steps.
learningGoals: Use diff views to separate unrelated edits before staging.
Create focused commits with meaningful messages.
Read the finished history and explain what changed in each snapshot.

projectBrief.scenario: Volunteer Hub already has the clean Module 1 snapshot. Add a supplied page and supplied style change as separate project purposes.
projectBrief.role: Act as the maintainer protecting a readable project history.
projectBrief.workspace: Continue the same volunteer-hub repository and history from Module 1.
projectBrief.deliverable: Three total commits: Start volunteer hub, Add volunteer signup page, and Add volunteer page styles, with a clean working tree.
requirements: Use the exact supplied index.html and styles.css text.
Inspect unstaged changes before staging.
Stage and commit the page separately from styles.
Inspect the resulting three-commit history.

stepLadder.title: Edit the Signup Heading
stepLadder.requirement: Replace the exact supplied heading line in volunteer-hub/index.html, then inspect that unstaged change.

stepLadder.title: Commit the Signup Page
stepLadder.requirement: Stage only index.html and commit Add volunteer signup page.

stepLadder.title: Edit the Footer Spacing
stepLadder.requirement: Replace the exact supplied footer line in volunteer-hub/styles.css, then inspect that unstaged change.

stepLadder.title: Inspect the Proposed Style Snapshot
stepLadder.requirement: Stage styles.css and inspect the staged diff before committing.

stepLadder.title: Commit the Styles
stepLadder.requirement: Commit Add volunteer page styles without changing the earlier commits.

stepLadder.title: Audit the Focused History
stepLadder.requirement: Inspect the three-commit history, inspect HEAD, and confirm clean state.

sections.description: Continue the same volunteer-hub repository and reapply Module 2 without copying the guided community-site task.

modules.title: Maintain a Clean Repository
modules.description: Continue community-site by changing tracked paths safely, ignoring generated output, and recovering from staged or unstaged mistakes without rewriting history.
modules.purpose: Teach day-to-day repository maintenance while preserving the meaningful history built in Modules 1 and 2.
learningObjectives: Rename and remove tracked files through Git and inspect the staged structural change.
Write exact ignore rules for generated output and stop tracking a generated file safely.
Restore an unstaged edit and unstage a proposed change without losing the tracked file.
Finish with intentional tracked files, ignored artifacts, and a clean working tree.
guidedExercises: Rename and remove supplied community-site paths without inventing file content.
Add exact .gitignore text and verify generated files are excluded.
Create and recover supplied temporary mistakes at the working-tree and staging layers.
modules.moduleProject: Continue volunteer-hub from Module 2 and prepare one clean release snapshot through six cumulative steps.

sections.title: From Tracked Changes to Clean Handoff
sections.summary: Manage a project whose files are changing names and purposes without losing the history that explains them.

topics.title: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
topics.summary: Continue community-site by moving and deleting tracked paths through Git, then inspect the staged structural change.
learningGoals: Rename a tracked community-site file with git mv.
Remove an obsolete tracked file with git rm.
Inspect staged structural changes before committing them.

topics.title: Keep Generated Files Out of History
topics.summary: Add exact ignore rules to community-site and stop tracking generated output without deleting the learner’s useful source files.
learningGoals: Add the exact supplied patterns to community-site/.gitignore.
Verify generated output is ignored while source files remain trackable.
Stop tracking an already-tracked generated file without deleting the working copy.

topics.title: Recover from Everyday Mistakes
topics.summary: Recover prepared working-tree and staging mistakes in community-site while preserving its existing commit history.
learningGoals: Restore an unstaged community-site edit from the current committed snapshot.
Remove a file from the staging area while keeping its working copy.
Recover a staged mistake safely without rewriting commit history.

sections.description: Continue community-site by changing tracked paths safely, ignoring generated output, and recovering from staged or unstaged mistakes without rewriting history.

sections.title: Parallel Module Project
sections.summary: Finish volunteer-hub with tracked path maintenance, exact ignore rules, safe recovery, and a clean handoff commit.

topics.title: Module Project: Clean the Volunteer Hub Repository
topics.summary: Continue volunteer-hub from Module 2 and finish a clean release snapshot through six cumulative maintenance steps.
learningGoals: Use Git-aware rename and removal commands for tracked files.
Create an effective .gitignore rule and verify ignored artifacts.
Restore staged and unstaged mistakes without rewriting commit history.

projectBrief.scenario: Volunteer Hub has the focused Module 2 history. Prepare it for handoff by maintaining tracked paths, adding ignore rules, and creating then recovering one supplied mistake safely.
projectBrief.role: Act as the maintainer preparing a clean local release snapshot.
projectBrief.workspace: Continue the same volunteer-hub repository and history from Modules 1 and 2.
projectBrief.deliverable: A clean four-commit repository with pages/signup.html, no obsolete notes file, generated output ignored, and final commit Prepare volunteer hub handoff.
requirements: Rename index.html to pages/signup.html through Git.
Remove notes/schedule.txt through Git.
Add exact generated/ and *.log ignore rules.
Create the exact supplied README mistake, then recover it safely.
Commit Prepare volunteer hub handoff and verify clean state.

stepLadder.title: Rename the Tracked Page
stepLadder.requirement: Create pages/ if needed and rename index.html to pages/signup.html with git mv.

stepLadder.title: Remove the Obsolete Schedule
stepLadder.requirement: Remove notes/schedule.txt with git rm and inspect the staged structural summary.

stepLadder.title: Add Exact Ignore Rules
stepLadder.requirement: Create .gitignore containing generated/ and *.log on separate lines; do not invent other patterns.

stepLadder.title: Keep Generated Output Out of History
stepLadder.requirement: Verify generated/preview.html and debug.log are ignored, then stage .gitignore with the tracked structural cleanup.

stepLadder.title: Recover the Prepared README Mistake
stepLadder.requirement: Unstage and restore only the prepared accidental README.md edit while preserving the intentional cleanup.

stepLadder.title: Commit and Verify the Handoff
stepLadder.requirement: Commit Prepare volunteer hub handoff, inspect the four-commit history, and confirm clean state.

sections.description: Continue the same volunteer-hub repository and reapply Module 3 without copying the guided community-site task.

modules.title: Final Capstone
modules.description: Start a fresh neighborhood-guide repository and independently reapply the complete local Git workflow through six cumulative steps.
modules.purpose: Prove transfer: the learner receives exact file edits but must choose and apply the Git workflow independently.
learningObjectives: Initialize and configure a fresh local repository.
Create focused commits from supplied file changes.
Inspect history and maintain tracked paths safely.
Ignore generated output, create and recover a supplied temporary mistake, and verify a clean handoff.
guidedExercises: Complete one six-step cumulative capstone with exact file-edit instructions and progressively less Git guidance.
modules.moduleProject: Build and verify the neighborhood-guide history through six cumulative steps.

sections.title: Final Capstone
sections.summary: Build and hand off one complete local history for a neighborhood guide.

topics.title: Final Capstone: Neighborhood Guide History
topics.summary: Start a fresh neighborhood-guide repository and independently build a clean, readable local history through six cumulative steps.
learningGoals: Initialize and grow one repository through a sequence of purposeful commits.
Separate content changes from release cleanup work.
Use status, diff, staging, restore, ignore, rename, removal, log, and show together.

projectBrief.scenario: A neighborhood team needs a trustworthy local history for a fresh guide. Build it from an ordinary folder to a clean handoff.
projectBrief.role: Act as the sole maintainer applying the complete local Git workflow independently.
projectBrief.workspace: Use neighborhood-guide for all six cumulative capstone steps. It does not reuse community-site or volunteer-hub history.
projectBrief.deliverable: A clean four-commit repository with focused history, maintained paths, exact ignore rules, and no tracked generated output.
requirements: Initialize and configure neighborhood-guide locally.
Use exact supplied file text rather than inventing web content.
Create focused commits with the authored messages.
Rename and remove tracked paths safely.
Ignore generated output, then create and recover a supplied temporary mistake.
Verify final history, files, ignore behavior, and clean state.

stepLadder.title: Initialize and Record the Baseline
stepLadder.requirement: Initialize neighborhood-guide on main, configure the supplied local identity, stage the authored baseline files, and commit Start neighborhood guide.

stepLadder.title: Add the Services Page
stepLadder.requirement: Create pages/services.html with the exact supplied text, inspect it, and commit Add local services page.

stepLadder.title: Update Contact Hours
stepLadder.requirement: Replace the exact hours line in pages/contact.txt with 9 AM - 5 PM, inspect it, and commit Update help desk hours.

stepLadder.title: Add the Ignore Policy
stepLadder.requirement: Create .gitignore with generated/ on one line and verify generated/preview.html is ignored.

stepLadder.title: Clean Tracked Paths and Recover the Mistake
stepLadder.requirement: Rename pages/welcome.html to pages/getting-help.html with git mv, remove notes/old-phone-tree.txt with git rm, create the exact supplied README.md mistake, then unstage and restore it.

stepLadder.title: Commit and Audit the Final Handoff
stepLadder.requirement: Commit Prepare neighborhood guide handoff, inspect the four-commit history and HEAD, and confirm clean state.

sections.description: Build one fresh neighborhood-guide repository through six cumulative steps.

projectJourneys.title: Community Site guided repository
projectJourneys.purpose: Teach each Git idea inside one repository that grows from an ordinary folder into a clean, readable local history.

projectJourneys.title: Volunteer Hub parallel repository
projectJourneys.purpose: Reapply each module’s essential Git workflow in a different repository with less command-level guidance while preserving one cumulative project across all module projects.

projectJourneys.title: Neighborhood Guide independent repository
projectJourneys.purpose: Prove independent transfer by rebuilding the complete local Git workflow in a fresh repository and carrying all six capstone steps forward.
